did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper describes the development and validation of artificial intelligence-driven detection models for basal cell carcinoma (a dermatology/skin cancer task) using large-scale digital pathology datasets compiled via the HoBBIT system. The abstract and introduction explicitly mention these models as part of the research applications facilitated by the platform.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is labeled 'Research and Applications' in the Journal of the American Medical Informatics Association. It describes the original development, implementation, and operational validation of a holistic digital pathology solution (including the MSK Viewer and HoBBIT system) at Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center. The study reports original results from a 3-year operational period involving 926 pathologists and 288,903 digital slides, and details the development and validation of AI-driven detection models for prostate cancer, basal cell carcinoma, and breast cancer metastases.

title: Integrated digital pathology at scale: A solution for clinical diagnostics and cancer research at a large academic medical center
journal: Journal of the American Medical Informatics Association
brief_summary: This study describes the development and operational validation of a holistic digital pathology solution at Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, integrating a vendor-agnostic viewer and the HoBBIT system for large-scale dataset compilation. The platform facilitated the creation of artificial intelligence-driven detection models for basal cell carcinoma, prostate cancer, and breast cancer metastases using thousands of cases. These AI models were developed and validated on large-scale digital pathology datasets comprising anonymized images, redacted reports, and clinical data. The solution was operationally used over three years by 926 pathologists and researchers who evaluated 288,903 digital slides. Seamless integration into hospital systems increased adoption, with 51% of slides reviewed within one month of scanning. The study concludes that interconnected digital pathology solutions enhance clinical diagnostics and facilitate next-generation computational pathology research.

lesion_types_studied.details: AI-driven detection models for prostate cancer were developed and validated on thousands of cases using the HoBBIT system.
AI-driven detection models for basal cell carcinoma were developed and validated on thousands of cases using the HoBBIT system.
AI-driven detection models for breast cancer metastases were developed and validated on thousands of cases using the HoBBIT system.

all_task_types.target: Presence of specific cancer types (prostate cancer, basal cell carcinoma, breast cancer metastases)
all_task_types.details: The paper describes the development and validation of 'artificial intelligence–driven detection models' for prostate cancer, basal cell carcinoma, and breast cancer metastases. These models predict the presence of specific diagnostic categories (lesions/cancers) within digital pathology slides, which constitutes a classification task (specifically, detection/classification of disease presence).

datasets_used.name: MSKCC Digital Pathology Cohort (HoBBIT-derived)
datasets_used.description: A large-scale digital pathology dataset compiled from Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center (MSKCC) clinical workflows using the HoBBIT system. It includes anonymized whole slide images (WSI), redacted pathology reports, and clinical data of patients with consent. The cohort consists of slides scanned from routine anatomic pathology and archival collections using multiple scanner models (Leica AT2, Leica GT450, 3DHistech P1000, Philips UFS).

use.training: Used to develop artificial intelligence-driven detection models for prostate cancer, basal cell carcinoma, and breast cancer metastases.
use.validation: Used to validate artificial intelligence-driven detection models for prostate cancer, basal cell carcinoma, and breast cancer metastases.
use.other: Operational use by 926 pathologists and researchers for clinical diagnostics and research over 3 years.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper describes the development and validation of AI models and the operational implementation of a digital pathology platform but does not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests for the AI model performance or platform adoption metrics.

MSKCC Digital Pathology Operational Coho.notes: The platform was operationally used over 3 years. 51% of slides were reviewed within 1 month of scanning. 79% were opened within 6 months. 97% of routine glass slide requests were obviated by direct access to digital slides. 3.6 million glass slides were digitized at a rate of 140,000 slides/month. Pathologists reported 31 scans for blurri-ness, 7 for missing tissue, 5 for air bubbles, and 7 for other reasons in 2018 and 2019.

Prostate Cancer Detection Model.notes: An AI-driven detection model for prostate cancer was developed and validated on thousands of cases using the HoBBIT system. Specific quantitative performance metrics (e.g., AUC, sensitivity, specificity) were not reported in the provided text.

Basal Cell Carcinoma Detection Model.notes: An AI-driven detection model for basal cell carcinoma was developed and validated on thousands of cases using the HoBBIT system. Specific quantitative performance metrics were not reported in the provided text.

Breast Cancer Metastases Detection Model.notes: An AI-driven detection model for breast cancer metastases was developed and validated on thousands of cases using the HoBBIT system. Specific quantitative performance metrics were not reported in the provided text.

Clinical Digital Workflow.scope: top-line diagnosis, margin status, lymphovascular and/or perineural invasion, pathology stage, and the need to order ancillary testing

Digital Sign-out Equivalency Study.notes: A prospective digital pathology concordance study including remote review of patient pathology resulted in 100% major diagnostic concordance. This led to provisional approval from the New York State Department of Health for primary diagnosis and remote sign-out.

architecture_details.description: The paper mentions the development of 'artificial intelligence–driven detection models' for prostate cancer, basal cell carcinoma, and breast cancer metastases. It states that the high-performance computing cluster enables 'training of deep neural network models' but does not specify the architecture, model family, backbone, layers, heads, or patch size of these AI models.
architecture_details.infrastructure: High-performance computing cluster comprising 7 NVIDIA DGX-1 nodes with 8 Tesla V100 GPUs each, 6 servers with 12 GTX 1080Ti GPUs and 12 Titan X GPUs, and 512 GB RAM.

training_details.details: Specific training hyperparameters, loss functions, optimizers, or schedules were not reported in the provided text.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: artificial intelligence–driven detection models for prostate cancer
artificial intelligence–driven detection models for basal cell carcinoma
artificial intelligence–driven detection models for breast cancer metastases
preprocessing_techniques: Manual postprocessing after scanning: verification of macro images by technicians for obvious artifacts.

De-identification of Whole Slide Images (WSI) for research datasets: removal of Protected Health Information (PHI) from label stickers, macro images, and text metadata (barcode, accession ID, scanning dates) by overwriting corresponding data.

De-identification of pathology reports: application of date truncation (removing day and month, leaving year), ID generation (creating novel de-identified HoBBIT identifiers for patient, case, and image IDs), and text redaction (replacing identifiers with placeholder text using vendor software De-Id and a homegrown regular expression algorithm).
Automated detection of dark images to exclude PHI in the main high-resolution image data (specifically for label stickers scanned close to tissue).
conclusions: Building interconnected digital pathology solutions increases the adoption of digital pathology by seamlessly integrating into clinical workflows and hospital systems.
Interconnected solutions facilitate next-generation computational pathology at scale for enhanced cancer research.
The developed platform serves as a blueprint and provides orientation for other pathologists regarding the challenges and lessons learned when implementing digital pathology at scale.
Holistic integration of digital pathology into institutional landscapes is crucial for broad adoption, facilitating clinical workflows, large-scale research, and educational programs.
important_remarks: The paper does not report specific quantitative performance metrics (e.g., AUC, sensitivity, specificity) for the AI-driven detection models for prostate cancer, basal cell carcinoma, or breast cancer metastases, only stating they were 'developed and validated on thousands of cases'.

The study is limited to a single academic medical center (Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center), which may limit the generalizability of the findings to other institutions with different workflows or scanner landscapes.

The AI models were developed using a heterogeneous scanner landscape (Leica AT2, Leica GT450, 3DHistech P1000, Philips UFS), which introduces potential domain shift challenges not explicitly quantified in the provided text.
There is a potential conflict of interest as some authors are founders or consultants for Paige, a company involved in AI pathology, which may introduce bias in the presentation of AI capabilities.

The digital sign-out equivalency study reported 100% major diagnostic concordance, but this was based on a prospective study with remote review; the sample size and specific statistical power for this concordance are not detailed in the summary.

The operational validation relies on self-reported or system-logged usage data (e.g., 926 users, 288,903 slides) over 3 years, which reflects adoption but does not independently verify diagnostic accuracy or clinical outcomes.

Data de-identification processes relied on automated detection of dark images and manual verification, which may have residual risks of PHI leakage if the automated detection fails or manual verification is inconsistent.

The study highlights that seamless integration into existing hospital systems (AP-LIS, cBioPortal, MPath, PPBC) was crucial for adoption, suggesting that standalone digital pathology solutions may face significant deployment barriers.
